did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)-based U-Net algorithm for skin lesion segmentation and employs machine learning classifiers (K-Nearest Neighbors, Random Forest, and Support Vector Machine) for melanoma classification in dermoscopy images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, including the proposal of novel feature descriptors (INSCF, INMOP, INSCMOP), the use of a U-Net algorithm for segmentation, and the application of classifiers (KNN, RF, SVM) on specific datasets (ISIC 2016, 2017, 2019) to achieve new classification results. It contains sections on methods, experimental evaluation, and results, distinguishing it from a review paper.

title: Melanoma classification employing inter neighbor statistical color and mean order pattern texture feature
brief_summary: This study proposes an automated melanoma classification system using novel inter-neighbor statistical color (INSCF) and mean order pattern texture (INMOP) features, combined as INSCMOP. The methodology employs a U-Net convolutional neural network for skin lesion segmentation followed by feature extraction and classification using K-Nearest Neighbors, Random Forest, and Support Vector Machine classifiers. The approach was evaluated on the ISIC 2016, ISIC 2017, and ISBI 2019 dermoscopy image datasets using 10-fold cross-validation. The proposed INSCMOP features with the Random Forest classifier achieved the highest performance, yielding accuracies of 93.27%, 86%, and 92.31% for the respective datasets. The results demonstrate that incorporating inter-chromatic texture patterns significantly improves classification accuracy compared to existing methods.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target class for binary classification (Melanoma vs. Benign) across ISIC 2016, ISIC 2017, and ISBI 2019 datasets.
Aggregate class used as the negative class in binary classification tasks. Composed of various specific benign types depending on the dataset.
Specific benign class present in ISIC 2017 (1372 images) and ISBI 2019 datasets, grouped into the 'benign' category for the study's binary classification task.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into 'Melanoma' (malignant) or 'Benign' (non-melanoma) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper employs K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Random Forest (RF), and Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers to predict discrete diagnostic labels (benign vs. melanoma) using extracted color and texture features. The task is evaluated on ISIC 2016, ISIC 2017, and ISBI 2019 datasets using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2016 (ISBI 2016)
datasets_used.description: A public challenge dataset of skin lesion analysis for melanoma detection released by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC). It contains dermoscopy images of skin lesions categorized into melanoma and benign (non-melanoma).

use.training: Used for segmentation training (90% of Part 1) and classification training (Part 3 images via 10-fold cross-validation).
use.validation: Used for segmentation testing (10% of Part 1) and classification via 10-fold cross-validation.
use.testing: Used for classification evaluation via 10-fold cross-validation.
use.other: Part 1 ground truth was held out for segmentation support.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017 (ISBI 2017)
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2017 challenge. It provides skin lesion images with masks for segmentation and annotations for classification. The dataset includes three specific categories which were aggregated into binary classes for this study.
classes_used: Benign (Aggregated from Seborrheic keratosis and Nevus)

use.training: The ISIC 2016 dataset was used as the training data to classify these images.
use.testing: Used for testing the classification model. Images were grouped into two categories: 374 Melanoma images and 1626 Benign images (254 Seborrheic keratosis + 1372 Nevus).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 (ISBI 2019)
datasets_used.description: A comprehensive database released as a new version of previous ISBI databases. It includes nine different types of skin lesions. For this study, 2000 samples were selected, divided equally into melanoma and non-melanoma groups.

use.training: Used for classification evaluation via 10-fold cross-validation.
use.validation: Used for classification evaluation via 10-fold cross-validation.
use.testing: Used for classification evaluation via 10-fold cross-validation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used for training and testing data evaluation on the ISIC 2016 dataset.
Used to quantitatively assess the performance of the U-Net segmentation algorithm.
Used to evaluate and compare the classification performance of the proposed method against other classifiers and state-of-the-art methods.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and Dice-coefficiency, with comparisons made based on percentage improvements over other methods rather than statistical significance.

classification.notes: The proposed INSCMOP with Random Forest classifier achieved the highest performance. It showed +17.31% accuracy improvement over KNN and +13.46% over SVM. Compared to previous works, it showed +3.85% over INMOP-RF, +11.54% over INSCF-RF, +14.26% over existing hand-crafted features with KNN, +8.08% over SVM, and +10.39% over RF classifier. Compared to other state-of-the-art methods, it achieved +11.48% over Inception-ResNetv2, +6.77% over SE-Inception-ResNetv2 & Inception-ResNetv22, +3.07% over combined ResNet50 and ResNet101, +11.94% over VGG16, +10.18% over CNN with SVM, and +7.77% over DRN-50.

The proposed method outperformed other works: +2.2% over ensemble of deep CNN, +0.3% over FCRN and Lesion index calculation, +2.8% over fine-tuned ResNet50 with SVM, and +0.7% over Hybrid features with SVM. Other methods compared included SVM [19] with 85.3% accuracy, 81.28% precision, and 86.22% F1-score.
Random Forest classifier produced the maximum Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-score among the tested methods on this dataset.

architecture_details.type: Deep Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for segmentation
architecture_details.contracting_path: Convolutional blocks followed by Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) and max-pooling layer
architecture_details.feature_maps: 32 feature maps in the first block; doubles after each block
architecture_details.expanding_path: Convolutional layers only (no max-pooling); upsampling layers combined with features from contracting path

training_details.dataset_split: 90% of Part 1 (900 images) used for segmentation training; 10% for testing

preprocessing_techniques: Skin lesion segmentation using a Convolutional Neural Network-based U-Net algorithm to isolate the lesion region from healthy skin

Feature extraction from segmented images using Inter Neighbor Statistical Color Feature (INSCF), Inter Neighbor Mean Order Pattern (INMOP), and Inter Neighbor Statistical Color Mean Order Pattern (INSCMOP)
Utilization of inter-channel chromatic information (RGB) for feature calculation
conclusions: The proposed method, utilizing Inter Neighbor Statistical Color Mean Order Pattern (INSCMOP) features with a Random Forest classifier, achieved the highest classification performance among the tested methods.
The system achieved accuracies of 93.27% on the ISIC 2016 dataset, 86% on the ISIC 2017 dataset, and 92.31% on the ISBI 2019 dataset.

Incorporating inter-chromatic texture patterns and diagonal pixel information from inter-color channel values significantly improves classification accuracy compared to existing hand-crafted features and state-of-the-art deep learning methods.

The proposed approach outperformed previous works, showing accuracy improvements of +11.48% over Inception-ResNetv2, +6.77% over SE-Inception-ResNetv2 & Inception-ResNetv22, and +3.07% over combined ResNet50 and ResNet101 on the ISIC 2016 dataset.

The automated classification process requires no manual interaction, providing a valuable assistance for dermatologists in clinical practice by lowering mortality rates through early and accurate diagnosis.
The U-Net convolutional neural network algorithm effectively segments skin lesion areas, enabling feature extraction only from the relevant lesion regions rather than the whole image.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation; the model was trained on ISIC 2016 and tested on ISIC 2017 and ISBI 2019, which may limit generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the performance improvements over state-of-the-art methods, relying instead on percentage point differences.
The ISIC 2017 dataset was used exclusively for testing, while ISIC 2016 was used for training, creating a potential domain shift between training and testing distributions.
The ISBI 2019 dataset evaluation used a pre-selected subset of 2000 samples (1000 melanoma, 1000 non-melanoma) rather than the full available dataset, which may introduce selection bias.
The 'Benign' class in ISIC 2017 is an aggregate of Seborrheic Keratosis and Nevus, potentially masking class-specific performance issues within benign lesions.
The U-Net segmentation was trained on only 90% of Part 1 of the ISIC 2016 dataset (900 images), which is a relatively small training set for a deep learning segmentation model.
The paper claims clinical relevance for lowering mortality rates but provides no clinical workflow integration, user study, or prospective validation data to support this claim.

No data leakage risks are explicitly addressed, but the use of 10-fold cross-validation on ISIC 2016 and ISBI 2019 requires careful verification that no patient data overlaps between folds or with the ISIC 2017 test set.
